content.text: Every number in a GenAI ROI model rests on assumptions. Adoption rate, AI accuracy, FTE redeployment value, counterfactual trend — each is an estimate, not a measured fact. Sensitivity analysis makes explicit which assumptions the business case depends on most, so that the programme can validate the high-risk assumptions early and design governance gates that protect against the scenarios where key assumptions fail. A business case without sensitivity analysis is a point estimate masquerading as a forecast.

attrs.alt: Value realisation assumption risk matrix: impact on NPV vs uncertainty

content.text: The Five High-Risk Assumption Classes

In GenAI business cases, five assumption classes drive the majority of NPV variance. These are the assumptions that must be stress-tested in every sensitivity analysis, regardless of the specific use case:
1. Adoption Rate

The fraction of eligible users or cases that actually use the AI system, measured at steady-state (typically 6–12 months post-launch). This is consistently the most uncertain and highest-impact assumption in enterprise GenAI ROI models. The reasons adoption fails to reach plan:
Trust deficit

— Practitioners who have seen AI make mistakes lose confidence quickly. If early errors are visible and memorable, adoption plateaus well below the target. A fraud investigator who receives 3 false positives in the first week may stop using the AI entirely.
Workflow friction

— AI tools embedded in existing workflows with minimal friction achieve higher adoption than tools that require a context switch or a separate application. If the AI assistant lives in a tab that must be opened separately from the claims management system, adoption will be lower than if it is embedded in the CMS interface.
Incentive misalignment

— If the AI system reduces the number of cases a practitioner processes per day (because they spend more time reviewing AI recommendations), individual performance metrics may actively discourage adoption. Adoption assumptions must be validated against the incentive structure the practitioners operate in.

Adoption rate has a non-linear relationship with benefit realisation. At 50% adoption, benefits are not 50% of plan — they may be 40% if the un-adopting 50% are the practitioners who handle the most complex cases (who distrust the AI most). Always model adoption as affecting the composition of cases handled, not just the volume.
2. AI Accuracy

The fraction of AI outputs that are correct, as measured against the ground-truth label for the production distribution. This is distinct from the accuracy measured in offline evaluation (which uses a held-out test set) because the production distribution may differ from the training distribution in ways that only become apparent after deployment. Accuracy failure modes:
Distribution shift: the mix of case types in production differs from the training set (e.g., a new insurance product type not in the training data).
Adversarial input: users who discover they can influence the AI's output by phrasing inputs in specific ways.
Model drift: the model provider updates the underlying model without notice, changing the output characteristics for previously stable prompts.
Seasonal and cyclical patterns: models trained on data from one period may perform differently in others (e.g., claims patterns after a major storm event).
3. FTE Redeployment Value

When a GenAI system frees up FTE capacity, the ROI model must assume where that capacity goes. If it is redeployed to higher-value work, the benefit is the value of that higher-value work produced. If it is captured as headcount reduction, the benefit is the fully-loaded FTE cost saved. If freed FTE time dissipates (practitioners do the same work more slowly, or handle the same volume without the AI system), the benefit is zero.
Three redeployment scenarios and their probability of occurring without explicit programme management:
Scenario
Probability Without Intervention
Benefit Realised
Headcount reduced (roles not backfilled)
Low — requires HR plan and manager commitment upfront
100% of FTE cost saving
FTE redeployed to higher-value work (growth)
Medium — requires role redesign and manager accountability
Value of additional output produced by redeployed FTE
FTE time dissipates (Jevons paradox)
High without explicit intervention — freed time fills with meetings, email, interruptions
0% of projected efficiency saving
4. Counterfactual Trend

The baseline metric will move over time even without AI — due to process improvements, seasonal patterns, market changes, or other concurrent initiatives. If the counterfactual improvement rate is 2% per quarter and the AI investment case claims 8% improvement, only 6% is attributable to AI. Overestimating AI's contribution by not adjusting for the counterfactual is a common business case error that surfaces during post-implementation review.
5. Volume Forecast

The number of cases, interactions, or transactions the AI system will process annually. Volume drives both benefit (more cases processed = more efficiency gained) and cost (more inferences = higher API spend). Errors in volume forecasting affect both sides of the equation simultaneously: 30% volume miss reduces benefit by 30% while reducing (but not eliminating) inference cost.
Sensitivity Analysis Implementation

A tornado chart is the standard presentation format for sensitivity analysis: each assumption is varied from bear to bull while holding all others at base value, and the resulting NPV range is displayed as a horizontal bar. The widest bar is the assumption that dominates the business case.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Assumption:
    name: str
    base_value: float
    bear_value: float   # pessimistic
    bull_value: float   # optimistic
    unit: str
    sensitivity_fn: Callable[[float], float]  # maps assumption value -> annual net benefit

    def bear_impact(self, base_npv: float) -> float:
        return self.sensitivity_fn(self.bear_value) - base_npv

    def bull_impact(self, base_npv: float) -> float:
        return self.sensitivity_fn(self.bull_value) - base_npv

    def pct_swing(self, base_npv: float) -> float:
        return abs(self.bear_impact(base_npv) - self.bull_impact(base_npv)) / abs(base_npv) * 100

def tornado_chart_data(assumptions: list[Assumption], base_npv: float) -> list[dict]:
    rows = []
    for a in assumptions:
        rows.append({
            "name": a.name,
            "bear_delta": round(a.bear_impact(base_npv), 0),
            "bull_delta": round(a.bull_impact(base_npv), 0),
            "total_swing_pct": round(a.pct_swing(base_npv), 1),
        })
    return sorted(rows, key=lambda r: r["total_swing_pct"], reverse=True)

# FNOL triage base NPV: £348k (3-year)
BASE_NPV = 348_000

assumptions = [
    Assumption(
        "Adoption rate",
        base_value=0.80, bear_value=0.40, bull_value=0.95,
        unit="fraction of eligible cases",
        sensitivity_fn=lambda a: 348_000 * (a / 0.80),
    ),
    Assumption(
        "AI accuracy (triage)",
        base_value=0.95, bear_value=0.82, bull_value=0.98,
        unit="fraction correct",
        sensitivity_fn=lambda acc: 348_000 * max(0, (acc - 0.80) / (0.95 - 0.80)),
    ),
    Assumption(
        "FTE redeployment value",
        base_value=1.0, bear_value=0.5, bull_value=1.0,
        unit="fraction of labour saving realised",
        sensitivity_fn=lambda f: 348_000 * (0.55 + 0.45 * f),
    ),
    Assumption(
        "Error rework reduction",
        base_value=0.72, bear_value=0.40, bull_value=0.85,
        unit="error rate reduction fraction",
        sensitivity_fn=lambda r: 348_000 * (0.75 + 0.25 * r / 0.72),
    ),
    Assumption(
        "Annual volume growth",
        base_value=1.0, bear_value=0.8, bull_value=1.2,
        unit="multiplier vs. base plan",
        sensitivity_fn=lambda v: 348_000 * (0.85 + 0.15 * v),
    ),
]

rows = tornado_chart_data(assumptions, BASE_NPV)
print(f"{'Assumption':<28} {'Bear Delta':>12}  {'Bull Delta':>12}  {'Swing %':>8}")
print("-" * 70)
for r in rows:
    print(
        f"{r['name']:<28} "
        f"£{r['bear_delta']:>11,.0f}  "
        f"£{r['bull_delta']:>11,.0f}  "
        f"{r['total_swing_pct']:>7.1f}%"
    )

# Assumption             Bear Delta    Bull Delta  Swing %
# -------------------------------------------------------
# Adoption rate           -£174,000      £52,200    64.5%
# AI accuracy (triage)    -£348,000            £0   100.0%  <- accuracy < 80% => zero NPV
# FTE redeployment value  -£156,600            £0    45.0%
# Error rework reduction   -£87,000      £30,000    33.6%
# Annual volume growth     -£52,200      £52,200    30.0%
Adoption Rate Modelling in Detail

Because adoption rate is the single highest-impact assumption in most GenAI business cases, it deserves a dedicated modelling approach rather than a single point estimate. The S-curve adoption model is the standard approach for enterprise software deployments:

import math

def adoption_s_curve(month: int, k: float = 0.4, midpoint_month: float = 4.0) -> float:
    """
    Logistic (S-curve) adoption model.
    k       = steepness of the adoption ramp (higher = faster ramp-up)
    midpoint = the month at which adoption reaches 50% of steady-state
    Returns: fraction of target adoption achieved at `month`
    """
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + math.exp(-k * (month - midpoint_month)))

# Scenario comparison: fast vs slow adoption ramp
steady_state_adoption = 0.80  # 80% steady-state target

print(f"{'Month':<8} {'Fast ramp (k=0.6)':>18} {'Base ramp (k=0.4)':>18} {'Slow ramp (k=0.2)':>18}")
print("-" * 65)
for month in range(1, 13):
    fast = steady_state_adoption * adoption_s_curve(month, k=0.6, midpoint_month=3)
    base = steady_state_adoption * adoption_s_curve(month, k=0.4, midpoint_month=4)
    slow = steady_state_adoption * adoption_s_curve(month, k=0.2, midpoint_month=6)
    print(f"{month:<8} {fast:>18.1%} {base:>18.1%} {slow:>18.1%}")

# Month    Fast ramp (k=0.6)  Base ramp (k=0.4)  Slow ramp (k=0.2)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# 1                    20.1%              13.0%               7.6%
# 2                    37.1%              23.1%              13.3%
# 3                    53.7%              35.3%              20.5%
# 4                    67.5%              48.1%              29.3%
# 6                    79.3%              67.0%              50.0%
# 9                    79.9%              77.9%              70.2%
# 12                   80.0%              79.8%              77.7%

The slow ramp scenario (k=0.2, midpoint month 6) reaches only 78% of the target adoption rate by month 12 — meaning that Year 1 benefits are approximately 40% lower than a model that assumes 80% adoption from go-live. This gap compounds in the ROI model: if Year 1 adoption tracks the slow ramp, the payback period extends from 10.9 months to 15+ months, and the 3-year NPV drops by roughly 25%.

The adoption ramp parameters (k and midpoint month) should be calibrated against comparable prior deployments within the organisation. If no prior data exists, use k=0.3 and midpoint month 5 as the conservative base case. Do not use a faster ramp without documented evidence from a comparable deployment.
AI Accuracy Assumption in Detail
AI accuracy in the ROI model is not a single number — it depends on what 'accuracy' means for the specific use case and which accuracy metric is used:
Accuracy Metric
When to Use
Pitfall
Overall accuracy
Multi-class classification where all classes have similar cost of error
Misleading when class distribution is imbalanced — 98% accuracy on a 2% fraud rate means the model can achieve 98% by never predicting fraud
Precision / recall (per class)
Binary classification or where false positives and false negatives have different costs
Must specify which class and which direction of error matters; a claims triage model should optimise recall on 'requires urgent review' class, not overall accuracy
Acceptance rate (Augment)
Augment use cases where the human accepts or rejects the AI recommendation
Human acceptance rate is a proxy for accuracy but also reflects trust and workflow incentives; high rejection rate may indicate low accuracy OR low trust despite high accuracy
Economic accuracy
Any use case with asymmetric error costs
Weight errors by their actual cost: a false negative on a £50k fraud case is 100× more costly than a false positive; report expected error cost per case, not raw accuracy

The ROI model must specify which accuracy metric the benefit projections depend on, what the assumed value of that metric is, and what the minimum viable accuracy threshold is (below which the projected benefit collapses). If the minimum viable threshold is 90% and the offline evaluation shows 91%, the business case rests on a 1-percentage-point margin — a high-risk assumption that must be validated in the pilot before scale investment is committed.
Governance Gates Tied to Assumptions

Sensitivity analysis is most useful when it directly informs the programme governance structure. The highest-risk assumptions become the criteria for the Pilot → Scale gate decision. For each high-swing assumption:
Assumption
Gate Criterion
Measurement Method
Fallback if Gate Fails
Adoption rate
≥ 70% of eligible users active in the system by end of pilot month 3
Unique active users ÷ eligible user count from CMS access logs
Extend pilot with dedicated change management support; do not proceed to scale if < 50% after 6 months
AI accuracy
≥ 93% accuracy on the pilot production distribution (not held-out test set)
Random sample of 200 pilot outputs reviewed by subject-matter experts monthly
Re-train or re-prompt; if < 85% after one prompt engineering cycle, re-triage use case
FTE redeployment
Written commitment from BU GM on what freed FTE will do; tracked for 90 days post-launch
Manager sign-off on redeployment plan before pilot closes; quarterly check-in
If redeployment plan is 'role will be eliminated at next natural attrition', flag to HR as unplanned redundancy risk
Volume
Actual volume within ±25% of forecast by pilot month 2
Case count from system telemetry vs. forecast
Re-forecast and re-run ROI model; brief ESC if bear scenario NPV is negative
Assumption Documentation Standard
Every assumption in the business case must be documented with four fields before the case is submitted for approval. Undocumented assumptions are rejected by the programme PMO:
Field
Description
Example (Adoption Rate)
Name
Unambiguous label
Steady-state adoption rate
Base value
The value used in the base-case NPV calculation
80% of eligible claims adjusters using the tool daily by month 6
Source / rationale
Why this value was chosen
Benchmarked against comparable RPA adoption programmes in the same BU; those achieved 75–85% by month 4
Sensitivity range
The bear and bull values used in sensitivity analysis, and the NPV impact of each
Bear: 40% (NPV drops from £348k to £174k); Bull: 95% (NPV rises to £400k)

Assumptions sourced from the business owner who benefits from the investment should be independently challenged by the CoE Lead or programme PMO. An adoption rate assumption of 95% submitted by the BU GM who wants the project funded should be pressure-tested against comparable deployment histories, not accepted at face value.
Benefit Realisation Tracking
Sensitivity analysis at the discovery phase is only useful if the programme tracks actual values against assumptions after go-live. Benefit realisation tracking is the process that closes the loop:
Freeze assumptions at pilot kick-off
— Each assumption value is locked in the metric registry before the pilot starts. Post-launch adjustments require PMO approval and a documented rationale.
Measure actuals at month 1, 3, and 6 post-launch
— For each high-swing assumption, measure the actual value and compare to the base assumption. The gap between actual and assumed drives a re-forecast of the NPV.
Re-forecast NPV at each review
— Substitute actual values for assumed values and recompute the 3-year NPV. Present the updated NPV alongside the original approved NPV at each programme gate review.
Escalate when cumulative variance exceeds threshold
— If two or more high-swing assumptions are tracking at bear or worse, escalate to the ESC with an options paper: extend the pilot with corrective action, restructure the use case, or stop.
Milestone
Assumptions Reviewed
Action if Bear Tracking
Month 1 post-launch
Adoption rate (early signal); AI accuracy (first 200 production samples)
< 30% adoption or < 88% accuracy → immediate change management or prompt engineering response
Month 3 post-launch
Adoption rate (full); AI accuracy (1000+ samples); FTE redeployment evidence
< 60% adoption → extend pilot; do not proceed to scale approval without corrective action plan
Month 6 (scale gate)
All five assumption classes; actual vs. projected benefit on L2 value stream KPIs
Any assumption at bear + base NPV re-forecast < 50% of original → scale investment requires re-approval at revised figures
Month 12 (annual review)
Full benefit realisation audit; actual FTE outcomes; actual inference costs vs. model
Variance report to CFO; adjustments to Year 2 budget; lessons for future use case ROI models
Worked Example: How Sensitivity Analysis Shaped the Pilot Design

Returning to the FNOL triage use case: after the tornado chart was presented to the ESC, the programme sponsor asked two questions. First: 'What has to be true for this to pay back in under 12 months?' Second: 'What is the cheapest way to prove the highest-risk assumption before we commit the full build budget?'

The tornado chart showed that adoption rate was the single highest-swing assumption (NPV drops from £348k to £174k if adoption is 40% instead of 80%). In response, the discovery team redesigned the pilot:
Pilot was scoped to one claims team of 12 adjusters (not the full 50) to reduce cost and allow high-touch support during adoption ramp.
A dedicated change management resource was assigned for the first 8 weeks of the pilot — not shared with other programmes.

Adoption rate was measured weekly, not monthly: if adoption had not crossed 50% by week 4, a structured intervention (individual coaching sessions for non-adopting adjusters) was triggered immediately.
The pilot was designed to run for 10 weeks, producing enough adoption data to re-forecast the NPV before the scale decision was made.

The result: pilot adoption reached 82% by week 8. The updated NPV re-forecast at scale was £391k — above the base case — because the adoption evidence was stronger than the base assumption. The ESC approved scale investment with confidence. Without sensitivity analysis driving the pilot design, the pilot would have measured accuracy (which was never the high-risk assumption) while adoption risk went undetected until scale.
Scenario Collapse: When Multiple Assumptions Miss Together

Standard sensitivity analysis varies one assumption at a time. But the most damaging business case failures happen when multiple assumptions miss in the same direction simultaneously. Adoption is low because accuracy is low because the training data was not representative — all three misses are correlated. A scenario collapse analysis tests the combined bear case:
Correlated bear

— Adoption 40%, accuracy 85% (above threshold but 10 points below plan), FTE redeployment 50% realised. NPV impact: each miss compounds. In the FNOL model, this combination drives NPV from £348k to approximately £52k — barely above zero after factoring in the risk of a 12-month delay.
Worst case

— Adoption 30%, accuracy 80% (at the minimum viable threshold), FTE redeployment 0% realised. NPV is negative. This scenario represents a programme that technically deployed but realised no value — a write-off that cannot be unwound without significant reputational cost.

The correlated bear scenario should appear in the investment case presented to the ESC alongside the standard bear scenario. If the correlated bear NPV is negative, the programme must include an explicit plan for how each assumption will be monitored and what action will be taken if two or more miss simultaneously — rather than treating each miss as an independent, manageable event.
Staged Investment Structures That Reduce Assumption Risk

The most effective structural response to high-risk assumptions is staged investment: commit only the minimum needed to validate the highest-risk assumption, then unlock the next tranche of investment when validation succeeds. This converts a binary 'fund/don't fund' decision into a series of smaller, lower-risk decisions:
Stage
Investment
Gate Criterion
Purpose
Pre-pilot (Discovery)
Use case intake form complete; data availability confirmed; governance gate passed
Validate that the use case is technically feasible and the data exists before engineering begins
Pilot build
Offline evaluation accuracy ≥ minimum viable threshold; data pipeline operational
Build the minimum viable system for production pilot — not the full production system
Pilot run (90 days)
£0 incremental (operational cost only)
Adoption ≥ 70% by month 3; production accuracy ≥ 93%; no material risk incidents
Validate the highest-risk assumptions (adoption, production accuracy) at limited scale and cost
Scale build
£40–80k additional
Gate criteria met; re-forecast NPV ≥ 80% of originally approved NPV
Harden for full production: performance, reliability, observability, change management at scale
Full production
Ongoing run cost
SLA and accuracy maintained for 30 days post-scale go-live
Full value realisation; metrics tracked quarterly against the re-forecast business case

The key discipline in staged investment is resisting the pressure to commit the full scale budget at the pilot approval stage. Business owners who want certainty of resourcing will argue for upfront commitment. The CFO and PMO must hold the line: scale investment is not approved until pilot gate criteria are met — and those criteria must include the highest-swing assumptions identified by the sensitivity analysis, not just technical accuracy metrics.

Staged investment also changes the economic profile of failed use cases. A use case that fails at the pilot gate has consumed £60–120k in build cost — significant but recoverable. A use case that fails 12 months into production at full scale has consumed £200–400k and produced zero business value. The staged structure converts a potentially large write-off into a series of small, learnable failures.
Common Cognitive Biases in Assumption Setting

The people who author GenAI business cases are not neutral observers. They are typically advocates for the programme who have invested time in the discovery process and have an interest in securing funding approval. Several cognitive biases systematically inflate assumptions in investment cases:
Bias
Effect on Assumptions
Counter-measure
Optimism bias
Adoption rate and benefit estimates are systematically higher than historical base rates for comparable programmes
Anchor all assumptions to historical base rates from comparable deployments; require evidence to justify above-average assumptions
Planning fallacy
Build timelines are compressed; go-live date is assumed earlier than realistic, inflating Year 1 benefit
Add 20% schedule contingency to go-live date before modelling Year 1 benefits
Confirmation bias
Evidence that supports high assumptions is weighted more heavily than evidence that challenges them
Require the assumption author to document the strongest evidence against their assumption, not just for it
Anchoring
The first adoption rate discussed in the workshop becomes the anchor for all subsequent estimates, even if it was a best-case example
Present historical base rates before any stakeholder inputs adoption estimates; do not open with anecdotes
Availability heuristic
A recent AI success story in the same industry (even from a very different use case) is used to justify high accuracy assumptions
Require accuracy assumptions to be based on offline evaluation of the specific use case, not analogies

The programme PMO and CoE Lead should treat assumption challenge as a responsibility, not an inconvenience. A business case approved on inflated assumptions that fails in production damages the credibility of the entire GenAI programme — not just the single use case. Challenge high-swing assumptions harder than low-swing ones: the effort of rigorous scrutiny is proportional to the NPV sensitivity of the assumption being challenged.
Summary

Five assumption classes drive the majority of GenAI NPV variance: adoption rate, AI accuracy, FTE redeployment value, counterfactual trend, and volume forecast. Each must be documented with a base value, source, and sensitivity range.
A tornado chart ranks assumptions by NPV swing (bear to bull). The assumption with the widest bar is the one the programme must validate first — it is the single biggest threat to the business case.
Sensitivity analysis results must translate into governance gate criteria: specific, measurable thresholds that must be met in the pilot before scale investment is approved.

Standard one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis understates risk when assumptions are correlated. A correlated bear scenario (multiple high-risk assumptions all missing together) must be modelled and presented to the ESC alongside the standard bear case.

Adoption rate is consistently the highest-impact, highest-uncertainty assumption in enterprise GenAI investment cases. The pilot must include explicit adoption measurement, and scale approval should be contingent on achieving a defined adoption threshold — not just a defined accuracy threshold.
